issue: New QdrantClient constructed on every search() call instead of being reused at module scope
evidence:
def search(...):
    model = get_model()
    ...
    client = QdrantClient(url=QDRANT_URL)   # line 52 — per-call
estimated_impact: Each search opens a fresh HTTP client/connection to Qdrant. ~5-30ms of client setup + new TCP/HTTP handshake per request that the model already cached avoids. On the search-docs MCP hot path (every search_project_docs call) this is pure waste; +10-30ms p50 per query, and risks socket churn under concurrency.
recommendation: Memoise the client like _MODEL: a module-level _CLIENT with a get_client() lazy singleton, or build it once in get_model(). QdrantClient is reusable and thread-safe for queries.

issue: BGE-M3 model lazy-loaded on first request (cold start) on the request/tool path
estimated_impact: First search_project_docs call after MCP/process start blocks for the full BGE-M3 load (~2-8s on CPU, ~1-3s on MPS/CUDA, plus model download on a cold cache). Whole interpreter is single-threaded sync, so that first call freezes the server. Subsequent calls are fine (cached).
recommendation: Warm the model at server startup (call get_model() in search-docs server.py __main__ before mcp.run(), or in ingest/startup hook) so the cost is paid once at boot, not on the first user-facing query. Document the warm-up in the runbook.

issue: getOrders returns the entire orders collection with no limit/pagination + populates user per doc
evidence:
const orders = await Order.find({}).populate('user', 'id name')
res.json(orders)
estimated_impact: Admin GET /api/orders loads ALL orders + embedded orderItems into memory and serializes them in one response. At 10k orders averaging ~2KB each that is ~20MB JSON built synchronously in the handler (JSON.stringify blocks the event loop) and ~20MB down the wire. Grows unbounded with order volume. Cross-mate (security): unauthenticated-volume DoS amplifier if admin token leaks.
recommendation: Add pageSize/pageNumber paging like getProducts (.limit().skip() + countDocuments), or a date-bounded query. Return {orders, page, pages}. Frontend OrderListScreen renders the full array, so it inherits the same unbounded cost.

issue: No index on Order.user despite Order.find({ user }) being a routine query (getMyOrders)
evidence:
getMyOrders: await Order.find({ user: req.user._id })  // orderController.js:139
orderModel: user field has ref but no `index: true`
estimated_impact: GET /api/orders/myorders does a full collection scan filtered by user. With 100k orders and no index: +200-800ms per call and rising linearly with collection size; with an index it is ~1-5ms. Same class of issue for any user-scoped order lookup.
recommendation: Add `index: true` to the order `user` field (or orderSchema.index({ user: 1, createdAt: -1 })). Mongoose creates it on model init. Note: dev DB is small so this is invisible locally — it only bites in production.

issue: Product search uses an unanchored case-insensitive $regex, which cannot use an index = full collection scan
estimated_impact: Every keyword search runs countDocuments + find, both as full scans (case-insensitive unanchored regex defeats any btree index). With 50k products: ~150-500ms per search, doubled because count and find each scan. Cross-mate (security): unanchored user-supplied regex is a ReDoS / scan-amplification vector — flag to security-mate.
recommendation: Add a MongoDB text index on name (productSchema.index({ name: 'text' })) and use $text search, OR anchor + escape the regex and add a case-insensitive collation index. Either way drop the separate countDocuments full-scan if a $text/$facet pipeline can return count+page in one query.

issue: features.json (14KB) is read from disk and JSON.parsed on every GET /api/feature-flags request with no in-memory cache
estimated_impact: FeatureDashboard polls this endpoint (initial load + every Refresh + after each Auto-Pilot mutation). Async read avoids blocking the event loop, but JSON.parse of 14KB is ~0.5-1ms of sync CPU on the event loop per call, plus a syscall round-trip per request. Qualitative on small file, but it scales with file growth and request rate.
recommendation: Cache the parsed object in module scope, invalidate via fs.watch on features.json (the MCP writes atomically via os.replace, so a watch on the path fires reliably). Falls back to re-read on watch error. This makes reads O(1) memory hits and removes per-request parse cost.

issue: Synchronous file read/write inside MCP tool handlers
evidence:
def _read_features(): with FEATURES_PATH.open('r') as f: return json.load(f)   # line 48
def _write_features(...): tempfile + json.dump + os.replace   # line 52-65
estimated_impact: Qualitative only. Over stdio transport (default, single client) this is fine — one request at a time. But the server also supports --transport http/sse for n8n; under HTTP with concurrent callers these sync reads/writes block the event loop / serialize all flag operations. The atomic os.replace write itself is correct.
recommendation: Acceptable for stdio. If the HTTP/SSE transport is used in production, move file I/O to a thread (anyio.to_thread / asyncio.to_thread) or accept serialized access explicitly. Document that the HTTP transport is single-writer.

issue: filtered/counts useMemo read statusOverrides through getStatus closure but the dep array is hand-patched with eslint-disable, hiding a stale-memo hazard
evidence: const filtered = useMemo(() => { ... getStatus(id, f.status) ... }, [entries, search, statusFilter, statusOverrides])  // getStatus not in deps; eslint-disable-next-line
estimated_impact: Qualitative. Today statusOverrides IS listed so it recomputes correctly, but getStatus/getTraffic are recreated every render and the memo silently depends on them via closure. Low risk now (≤25 flags), but the eslint-disable removes the guardrail; a future edit can produce stale filter counts. No measurable perf cost at current scale.
recommendation: Inline the status lookup inside the memo (statusOverrides[id] || f.status) instead of calling the closure, so the dependency is explicit and the eslint-disable can be removed. Keep the list small enough that virtualization is unnecessary.

issue: getUsers returns the full users collection with password hashes and no pagination
evidence:
const users = await User.find({})   // no .select('-password'), no limit
res.json(users)
estimated_impact: Qualitative on user count, but: (1) unbounded array like getOrders — grows with registrations; (2) password hashes are serialized into the response (cross-mate: security — data exposure). At 100k users this is a multi-MB response built sync in the handler.
recommendation: Add pagination (.limit/.skip) and .select('-password'). Flag the password-hash exposure to security-mate.

note: No fs.readFileSync / sync HTTP in Express request handlers; featureFlagsController correctly uses fs/promises (separate caching finding raised).
